Reference & Education Modules
🔥
Hood Cleaning
NFPA 96 · Semi-Annual · Dietary
Standard

NFPA 96 — Standard for Ventilation Control and Fire Protection of Commercial Cooking Operations

Frequency

Semi-annual minimum; quarterly if high-volume cooking

Scope

Hood, filters, grease traps, ductwork, and exhaust fan

Vendor Requirement

Must be a certified hood cleaning company. Written report must be left on-site with service sticker applied.

Common Deficiencies
  • Incomplete duct cleaning — only hood cleaned, ductwork missed
  • Missing service sticker on hood
  • No written report retained on-site
  • Vendor not certified
  • Grease buildup exceeding 1/8 inch
🔗 NFPA 96 Standard Page
🧯
Dietary Hood Fire Suppression
NFPA 96 / NFPA 17A · Semi-Annual
Standard

NFPA 96 / NFPA 17A — Wet Chemical Extinguishing Systems

Frequency

Semi-annual inspection and service by licensed fire suppression contractor

Scope

Ansul or equivalent wet chemical system, fusible links, pull station, nozzle coverage and positioning

Documentation

Contractor leaves a tagged inspection report. Retain on-site. Agent quantity verified at each inspection.

Common Deficiencies
  • Expired fusible links — must be replaced per manufacturer schedule
  • Missing or illegible pull station tag
  • Improper nozzle positioning after equipment moves
  • Low agent quantity
  • No current inspection report on file
🔗 NFPA 17A Standard Page
🚨
Emergency & Exit Sign Testing
NFPA 101 §7.9 & 7.10 · Monthly + Annual
Standard

NFPA 101 Life Safety Code — Sections 7.9 (Emergency Lighting) & 7.10 (Exit Signs)

Frequency

Monthly: 30-second functional test (push-button)
Annual: Full 90-minute discharge test

Scope

All battery-backed emergency lights and all illuminated exit signs throughout the facility

90-Minute Test Documentation

Record: date, who performed test, each unit tested, pass/fail result, any units flagged for repair. Keep on file for survey.

Common Deficiencies
  • Failed lamp or dead battery not replaced promptly
  • Exit sign blocked by signage or storage
  • Missing monthly test documentation
  • 90-minute test not performed or not documented
  • Units not included on master inventory list
🔗 NFPA 101 Standard Page
🔔
Fire Alarm System
NFPA 72 · Annual Contractor + Weekly/Monthly Visual
Standard

NFPA 72 — National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code

Frequency

Annual full inspection by licensed contractor; weekly/monthly visual walk by facility staff

Scope

Pull stations, smoke/heat detectors, notification appliances (horns/strobes), fire alarm control panel, monitoring connection

What to Look For (Staff Rounds)

Panel in normal status (no faults), pull stations unobstructed, detectors not painted or covered, notification devices visible and unblocked

Common Deficiencies
  • Devices out of test or not included in annual inspection
  • Monitoring gaps — system not connected to central station
  • No current inspection report on file
  • Painted or dusty smoke detectors
  • Pull stations blocked by furniture or equipment
🔗 NFPA 72 Standard Page
🌬️
Fire Dampers
NFPA 80 / NFPA 90A / NFPA 105 · 4-Year & 6-Year
Standard

NFPA 80 (fire dampers), NFPA 90A (HVAC), NFPA 105 (smoke/combination dampers)

Frequency

Fire dampers: every 4 years. Smoke and combination dampers: every 6 years per NFPA 105. First test within 1 year of installation.

Scope

All fire and smoke dampers at fire-rated wall and floor penetrations throughout the facility

Testing Procedure

Drop damper, verify full closure, inspect for damage and obstructions, reset, verify proper operation. Document each damper by ID tag number.

Common Deficiencies
  • Dampers that won't close — rust, debris, or physical obstruction
  • Obstructed or missing access panels — can't reach damper for testing
  • No testing record or damper inventory
  • Missing damper ID tags
  • Dampers not on testing schedule
🔗 NFPA 80 🔗 NFPA 105
🚪
Fire Doors
NFPA 80 · Annual
Standard

NFPA 80 — Standard for Fire Doors and Other Opening Protectives

Frequency

Annual inspection of all fire-rated door assemblies

Scope

Self-closing hardware, positive latching, clearances (max 3/4" at sides/top, 3/4" at bottom), frame integrity, glazing, coordinator function, hold-open devices

Who Can Inspect

A qualified person with knowledge of NFPA 80. Deficiencies requiring repair must be addressed by a qualified contractor.

Common Deficiencies
  • Door propped open with wedge, chair, or tied-back hardware
  • Broken or missing self-closer
  • Door does not latch positively when released
  • Damaged frame or door panel — holes, gaps, unauthorized modifications
  • Missing or illegible fire rating label
  • Coordinator malfunction on pair of doors
🔗 NFPA 80 Standard Page
🏃
Fire Drill Requirements
NFPA 101 · Quarterly per Shift
Standard

NFPA 101 Life Safety Code + accreditation body standards

Frequency

Quarterly per shift — all shifts must be covered annually (day, evening, and night shifts each get at least one drill per year)

R.A.C.E.

Rescue anyone in immediate danger · Alarm — activate the nearest pull station · Confine — close all doors · Extinguish if safe, or Evacuate

P.A.S.S.

Pull the pin · Aim at the base of the fire · Squeeze the handle · Sweep side to side

Common Deficiencies
  • Not covering all shifts — only day shift drills documented
  • Missing post-drill critique documentation
  • Staff unable to demonstrate RACE or PASS
  • No drill documentation or incomplete records
  • Drills not spread across quarters
🧯
Fire Extinguishers
NFPA 10 · Monthly Visual + Annual Maintenance
Standard

NFPA 10 — Standard for Portable Fire Extinguishers

Frequency

Monthly: Visual inspection by staff · Annual: Maintenance by certified vendor · 6-Year: Internal inspection + hydrotest

Placement Rules

Max 75-foot travel distance to any extinguisher. Mounted in visible, accessible location. Top of unit no higher than 5 feet. Clear space in front at all times.

Monthly Check Items

In correct location · Visible and unobstructed · Full charge (gauge in green) · Tamper seal intact · Inspection tag current · No damage or corrosion

Common Deficiencies
  • Missing units — not returned after use or discharge
  • Blocked or obstructed access
  • Annual inspection tag expired
  • Missing monthly initials on tag
  • Wrong extinguisher type for hazard class (e.g., no K-Class in kitchen)
🔗 NFPA 10 Standard Page
💧
Fire Sprinkler System
NFPA 13 / NFPA 25 · Weekly + Quarterly + Annual
Standard

NFPA 13 (installation) and NFPA 25 (inspection, testing & maintenance)

Frequency

Weekly: Control valve visual check · Quarterly: Waterflow/tamper alarm test · Annual: Full contractor inspection

18-Inch Rule

Nothing may be stored within 18 inches of any sprinkler head. This is a hard stop — cited on almost every survey where storage is present.

Control Valve Check

Verify all control valves are in the open position and sealed/supervised. A closed valve means no water to that section — this is a critical finding.

Common Deficiencies
  • Storage within 18 inches of a sprinkler head
  • Painted or corroded sprinkler heads — must be replaced
  • Closed control valve — most serious finding
  • No weekly valve inspection log
  • Escutcheon plates missing (cosmetic but surveyed)
🔗 NFPA 25 Standard Page
📋
NFPA 99 Healthcare Facilities Risk Assessments
NFPA 99 · As Required for Renovations & New Systems
Standard

NFPA 99 — Health Care Facilities Code

When Required

When new systems are installed, spaces are renovated, or occupancy/use changes. Must be documented before work begins.

Risk Categories

Category 1: Failure likely to cause major injury or death · Category 2: Failure may cause minor injury · Category 3: Non-patient areas · Category 4: Administrative/business only

ILSMs

Interim Life Safety Measures — required when life safety systems are impaired. Document activation date, measures taken, and restoration date.

Common Deficiencies
  • No documented risk assessment before construction or renovation
  • Wrong risk category assigned — clinical area categorized as Category 3
  • ILSMs not activated when fire alarm or sprinkler impaired
  • ILSM activation not documented with start and end dates
🔗 NFPA 99 Standard Page

OSHA Quick Reference — Healthcare
🩸
Bloodborne Pathogens
29 CFR 1910.1030 · Annual Training Required

Requires an Exposure Control Plan, engineering controls (sharps containers, needleless systems), PPE, hepatitis B vaccination offer, post-exposure evaluation, and annual training for all employees with occupational exposure. Healthcare workers most commonly cited for missing written plan, no annual training records, or inadequate sharps disposal.

Common Violations
  • No written Exposure Control Plan or not updated annually
  • Missing annual training documentation
  • Sharps containers overfilled beyond fill line
  • No post-exposure evaluation procedure documented
🔗 OSHA Bloodborne Pathogens
⚗️
Hazard Communication / GHS
29 CFR 1910.1200 · SDS & Label Requirements

Requires a written Hazard Communication Program, a current SDS for every hazardous chemical used, GHS-compliant container labels, and employee training on chemical hazards. The SDS library must be accessible to all employees at all times — electronic access is acceptable if always available.

Common Violations
  • Missing or outdated SDS for chemicals in use
  • Containers without GHS labels or secondary containers unlabeled
  • No written Hazard Communication Program
  • Employees unable to locate SDS during inspection
🔗 OSHA Hazard Communication
🦺
Personal Protective Equipment
29 CFR 1910.132 · Hazard Assessment Required

Requires a written workplace hazard assessment to determine necessary PPE. Employer must provide required PPE at no cost (except everyday clothing, non-specialty safety-toe footwear, and prescription eyewear in certain cases). Employees must be trained on when to use PPE, what PPE is required, how to properly don/doff, limitations, and care/maintenance.

Common Violations
  • No documented hazard assessment certification
  • PPE not provided for identified hazards
  • No training documentation for PPE use
  • PPE not maintained in sanitary/reliable condition
🔗 OSHA PPE Standard
🔒
Lockout / Tagout
29 CFR 1910.147 · Energy Control Program

Requires an Energy Control Program (written procedures for each piece of equipment), training for authorized and affected employees, and an annual periodic inspection of each energy control procedure. Any time maintenance or service is performed on equipment that could release hazardous energy — LOTO must be applied.

Common Violations
  • No written energy control procedures for specific equipment
  • Locks and tags not provided to authorized employees
  • No annual review of LOTO procedures
  • Employees performing maintenance without applying LOTO
🔗 OSHA LOTO Standard
🚶
Walking / Working Surfaces — Slips, Trips, Falls
29 CFR 1910.22 · Housekeeping & Maintenance

Requires all walking and working surfaces to be kept clean, dry, and free of hazards. Aisles and passageways must be marked and kept clear. Wet surfaces must be marked with warning signage. Regular housekeeping and preventive maintenance are the primary compliance tools.

Common Violations
  • Cluttered aisles and exits
  • Wet floors without wet floor signage
  • Damaged floor surfaces not repaired
  • Cords across walkways
🔗 OSHA Walking/Working Surfaces
⚡
Electrical Safety
29 CFR 1910.303 · GFCI & Extension Cord Rules

Extension cords are for temporary use only — never permanent wiring. GFCI protection required in wet or damp locations. Electrical panels must have 36 inches of clear space in front. Cover plates must be present on all outlets and junction boxes. No daisy-chaining of power strips.

Common Violations
  • Extension cords used as permanent wiring
  • Daisy-chained power strips
  • Blocked electrical panels
  • Missing outlet cover plates
  • No GFCI in wet areas (kitchens, janitor closets)
🔗 OSHA Electrical Safety
📊
Recordkeeping — OSHA 300 Log
29 CFR 1904 · Annual Posting Feb 1 – Apr 30

Employers with 10 or more employees must maintain OSHA 300 (injury/illness log), 300A (annual summary), and 301 (incident report) forms. The 300A summary must be posted in a visible location from February 1 through April 30 each year. Records must be retained for 5 years.

Common Violations
  • 300A summary not posted during required period
  • Recordable cases not entered within 7 days of knowledge
  • Records not retained for 5-year minimum
  • Unsigned 300A (must be signed by company executive)
🔗 OSHA Recordkeeping  🔗 OSHA Healthcare eTool
Security
Incident Log

No incidents recorded yet

Use the button above to record the first incident.

Security References
Workplace Violence Prevention
De-escalation & Reporting
Key Principles

Workplace violence in healthcare settings includes physical assaults, verbal threats, and harassment from patients, visitors, or coworkers. Prevention requires a zero-tolerance policy backed by reporting mechanisms and training.

De-Escalation Techniques (CPI Principles)
  • Maintain calm, non-threatening body language — stay at eye level
  • Use a low, steady voice; avoid arguing or raising your voice
  • Acknowledge feelings: "I can see you're frustrated"
  • Offer limited, concrete choices to restore sense of control
  • Never turn your back — maintain awareness of exits
  • Call for assistance early; do not try to manage alone if escalating
Reporting Obligations
  • All violent incidents must be reported via this module and facility incident reporting system
  • OSHA requires recording qualifying incidents on the 300 Log
  • Law enforcement contact is required for physical assaults — document case number
Code Pink — Infant/Child Abduction
Response Steps & Lockdown
IMMEDIATE RESPONSE STEPS
  1. Announce Code Pink overhead and via radio — include description of missing child and last known location
  2. Lock down all exits immediately — station staff at every door; no one exits without authorization
  3. Search the immediate area — check closets, bathrooms, stairwells, vehicles if applicable
  4. Call 911 immediately — do not delay for internal investigation
  5. Notify administration, security supervisor, and risk management
  6. Preserve any surveillance footage — do not erase or record over
  7. Document everything — times, who was notified, search areas cleared
Communication Checklist
  • Overhead PA announcement made
  • 911 contacted — case number documented
  • Administrator on call notified
  • Risk management notified
  • Parents/guardians notified by appropriate staff
  • All-clear announced only by designated authority
Active Threat — Run, Hide, Fight
Code Silver Response
🏃
RUN
Evacuate if a safe path exists. Leave belongings. Help others escape if possible.
🫷
HIDE
Find a room, lock/barricade the door, silence phones, stay out of sight.
✊
FIGHT
Last resort only. Act aggressively, improvise weapons, commit fully.
When Law Enforcement Arrives
  • Keep hands visible and empty — do not point or make sudden movements
  • Follow all instructions immediately
  • Do not stop officers to ask questions — exit as directed
  • Once safe, call 911 to report your location and status
Visitor Management Policy
Badge, Escort & Restricted Areas
Badge Requirements
  • All visitors must sign in at the front desk and receive a dated visitor badge
  • Badges must be visible at all times while on facility property
  • Badges are non-transferable and expire at end of visit or end of day
Escort Procedures
  • Visitors in clinical or restricted areas must be escorted by a staff member at all times
  • Contractors must check in with facilities before proceeding to work areas
  • Unescorted visitors observed in restricted areas should be politely redirected and reported
Restricted Areas
  • Pharmacy, OR, ICU, server rooms, mechanical spaces, and security offices require prior authorization
  • Signage must clearly identify restricted areas
  • Any unauthorized person in a restricted area — contact security immediately
Hazardous Materials
Regulatory Reference Modules
📄
SDS Portal — Safety Data Sheets
Search online or manage your local SDS library
COMMON HOSPITAL CHEMICALS:
LOCAL SDS LIBRARY — Upload PDF
🚛
DOT Hazardous Waste Transportation
49 CFR Parts 171–180 · Generator Training Required
Generator Status

VSQG: ≤100 kg/month · SQG: 100–1,000 kg/month · LQG: >1,000 kg/month. Status determines storage time limits and reporting requirements.

Training Requirement

All employees who prepare, handle, or offer hazardous materials for transport must have DOT hazmat training. Refresher every 3 years. Records retained 3 years after employment ends.

Manifest Requirements

Uniform Hazardous Waste Manifest (EPA Form 8700-22) required for off-site transport. Generator retains copy for 3 years minimum.

Common Healthcare Waste

Chemotherapy waste · Mercury devices · Batteries · Fluorescent lamps · Aerosols · Photographic chemicals

Common Violations
  • Employees handling hazmat shipments without documented DOT training
  • Missing or incomplete shipping papers
  • Improper labeling or marking of containers
  • Manifests not retained for required period
🔗 DOT PHMSA Training Resources  🔗 EPA Generator Regulations
💊
Pharmacy Waste Management
EPA RCRA · DEA Controlled Substance Waste
Container Color Guide

Blue lid: Non-hazardous pharmaceutical waste · Yellow lid: Hazardous pharmaceutical waste (P-listed and U-listed) · Black lid: RCRA hazardous waste

P-Listed Waste

Acutely hazardous pharmaceuticals — must be handled as RCRA P-listed waste. Examples: warfarin (if >0.3%), physostigmine, epinephrine. Strict storage limits apply.

Controlled Substance Waste

DEA Form 41 for destruction on-site. Reverse distributors require DEA Form 222. Authorized witness and documentation required. No drain disposal of controlled substances.

Sharps Waste

Proper sharps containers required. Vendor pickup with manifest. Never overfill containers. Trace chemo sharps go into chemo sharps containers — not regular sharps.

Common Violations
  • Wrong container color — hazardous waste in non-haz container
  • Controlled substance disposal without proper DEA documentation
  • P-listed waste stored beyond 1-year time limit (SQG/LQG)
  • Drain disposal of pharmaceuticals
🔗 EPA Pharmaceutical Waste Final Rule
📄
SDS (Safety Data Sheet) Management
GHS 16-Section Format · Must Be Accessible at All Times

An SDS must exist for every hazardous chemical in the workplace and must be immediately accessible to employees in their work area during their shift. Electronic access is permitted if employees have unimpeded access at all times. The 16-section GHS format is required for all new SDSs.

Key SDS Sections

§1 Identification · §2 Hazard ID · §4 First Aid · §5 Firefighting · §6 Accidental Release · §8 Exposure Controls/PPE · §11 Toxicology · §13 Disposal

Maintenance

Keep SDS current — update when formulations change. Remove SDSs for chemicals no longer in use. Know how to request an SDS from a vendor if one is missing.

🔗 OSHA SDS Guide
⚠️
Spill Response Procedures
Tiered Response — Know Your Level
Level 1 — Staff Cleanup

Small spill of known, non-hazardous substance. Staff trained to clean up using standard spill kit. PPE per SDS. Document in incident log.

Level 2 — Hazmat Team

Large spill, unknown substance, or any amount of chemotherapy or hazardous pharmaceutical. Evacuate area. Call Hazmat/EHS. Announce Code Orange if warranted. Do not attempt cleanup.

Spill Kit Locations

Identify and document spill kit locations in your facility. Staff must know where kits are. Inspect kits quarterly for contents and expiration.

Chemo Spill Special Rules

Any amount of chemotherapy spill requires chemo spill kit — not standard spill kit. Two-person cleanup minimum. Double-bag all waste in yellow chemo waste containers. Document exposure.

🫙
Compressed Gas Cylinder Safety
Storage · Handling · Transport · Disposal
Storage Rules

Cylinders must be secured upright with chains or straps at all times. Full and empty cylinders stored separately. Oxygen cylinders stored at least 20 feet from flammable gas cylinders or combustibles, or separated by a 5-foot non-combustible wall.

Transport

Always use an approved hand truck or cart designed for cylinders. Never roll, drag, or carry cylinders horizontally. Valve protection cap must be in place during transport. Never transport in an enclosed vehicle.

Handling

Open valves slowly. Never use cylinders as rollers or supports. Never drop or strike cylinders. Legibly mark all empty cylinders "MT" or "Empty." Remove regulators before storage or transport.

Disposal

Return empty cylinders to the vendor. Never attempt to refill, repair, or dispose of cylinders in regular waste. Cylinders with damaged valves should be set aside and vendor notified immediately.

Utilities & Power
NFPA 110 — Emergency Power Supply System (EPSS)
⚙️
Weekly Generator Inspection
NFPA 110 §8.3.4 · Every 7 days
Visual Checks

Fuel level ≥3/4 tank · Coolant level & color · Oil level via dipstick · Battery terminals (no corrosion) · No visible leaks · ATS in AUTO position · Control panel normal (no alarms)

Documentation

Record date, fuel level %, inspector name, any anomalies noted. File weekly. Retained minimum 3 years per NFPA.

Common Findings
  • Fuel below 3/4 (thermal degradation risk)
  • Corroded battery terminals (poor transfer response)
  • ATS not in AUTO (defeats automatic protection)
  • Alarm silence button left active from previous test
🏃
Monthly Generator Load Test
NFPA 110 §8.4.2 · Once per month · 30 min minimum
Requirements

Run generator under load (≥30% rated capacity) or no-load for minimum 30 minutes. Verify stable voltage & frequency, proper fuel consumption, no abnormal noise or vibration.

Acceptance Criteria

Voltage: ±10% of rated · Frequency: 60±1 Hz · Stable load · No alarms · Coolant temp <180°F · Battery charger functioning · Clean return to standby

Common Findings
  • Test <30 minutes (doesn't meet minimum)
  • Load <30% (wet stacking & carbon buildup)
  • Voltage sag under load (AVR issue)
  • No fuel consumption recorded
💪
Annual Load Bank Test
NFPA 110 §8.4.2 · 2-hour full load · Annual
Standard

Full-load (80–100% rated capacity) test for minimum 2 hours using portable load bank. Proves generator can handle peak facility demand. May defer no more than 30 days if load bank unavailable.

Documentation Required

Date & time · Load % applied · Voltage & frequency every 15 min · Fuel consumption rate · Coolant & oil temps · Any alarms · Test duration & pass/fail · Technician name & signature

Common Findings
  • Deferred >30 days (non-compliant)
  • Can't reach 80% load (insufficient capacity)
  • Test <2 hours (doesn't stabilize cooling)
  • No load bank documentation retained
🔄
Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS) Testing
NFPA 110 §8.4.6 · Monthly functional + Annual exercise
Monthly Functional Test

Verify ATS switches from utility to generator and back. Transfer time: <4 sec (life safety branch), <10 sec (non-critical). Confirm utility sensing detects outage & genset sensing detects return.

Annual Exercise

Full simulated outage: kill utility mains, confirm transfer in spec time, run under load, confirm retransfer. Document voltage levels, timing, load handling.

Common Findings
  • ATS in OFF or Manual mode
  • Transfer time >10 sec (delayed transfer)
  • No annual full exercise on record
  • Utility sensing threshold set too high
🔋
Battery System Testing
NFPA 110 §8.3.8 · Monthly visual + Annual load test
Monthly Visual

No corrosion on terminals · Proper water level (flooded cells) · No cracks or leaks · Charger voltage 27.6 ±0.5 VDC · Specific gravity 1.200–1.300 per cell

Annual Load Test

Discharge test under load; battery must sustain cranking amps without voltage drop below 10.5V. Recharge & verify charger returns to float. Replace if capacity <80%.

Common Findings
  • Green corrosion on terminals
  • Water level depleted (overcharging or age)
  • Charger set to wrong voltage
  • No annual load test performed
  • Battery age >5 years without replacement schedule
Generator & EPSS Test Log
Weekly, monthly, and annual tests

No records yet

NFPA 99 — Health Care Facilities Code
💨
Medical Gas System Testing
NFPA 99 Ch. 5 · Post-work + Annual verification
Required After Any Work

Zone valve actuation test · Alarm panel response to simulated leak · Outlet pressure & flow verification · System purge per NFPA guidelines · Annual piping pressure test at 1.5× operating pressure

Pressure/Flow Specs

O₂: 50±5 psi, ≥15 L/min · Vacuum: 80–120 mmHg, ≥40 CFM · N₂O: 45±5 psi · Compressed Air: 50±5 psi · All zone valves isolate properly · Alarm latches & resets correctly

Common Findings
  • Work performed but no testing documentation
  • Pressure outside ±5% tolerance
  • Zone valve doesn't fully isolate
  • No annual piping pressure test on record
⚡
Line Isolation Monitor (LIM) Circuits
NFPA 99 §6.3.2 · Monthly test required
Standard

Each isolated power system (OR, ICU, Cath Lab) requires monthly LIM test. Simulate fault: monitor must alarm when impedance drops below 2 mA threshold. Verify audible alarm, visual light, and annunciator.

Threshold & Specs

Alarm threshold: 2 mA ±20% · Audible alarm ≥70 dB · Visual indicator bright & legible · Annunciator at nurse station · Test circuit: 1.5 kΩ per phase to ground

Common Findings
  • Monthly test not performed/documented
  • Threshold set >2.5 mA (slower response)
  • Indicator light burned out
  • Annunciator location changed, not re-tested
📊
Risk Category Framework (Cat 1–4)
NFPA 99 · System Classification Guide
Category 1 — Life Support

Failure likely causes death: ventilators, defibrillators, patient monitors, IV pumps in critical care. Requires redundancy, highest inspection frequency, immediate repair.

Category 2 — Critical

Failure causes serious harm but not immediately life-threatening: surgical lights, imaging, lab analyzers, medical gas systems. High inspection frequency, timely repair.

Category 3 — Non-Critical

Failure causes minimal harm: office blood pressure cuff, thermometer, standard lights. Standard maintenance, repair within reasonable timeframe.

Category 4 — Not Medical

Not connected to patient care: building HVAC, office computers, decorative lighting, furniture. Standard building maintenance applies.

Legionella & Waterborne Pathogen Prevention
💧
Water Management Plan
CMS & ASHRAE 188-2018 · Required for all healthcare facilities
Core Elements

1. Risk Assessment: map all water systems (cooling towers, hot water, decorative fountains, humidifiers) · 2. Control Measures: treatment, flushing, temperature maintenance · 3. Monitoring: sampling & temp logs · 4. Corrective Actions: protocols for positive cultures or deviations

Responsibilities

Designate Water Management Program Lead · Facility risk assessment every 2 years · Annual plan review & updates · Staff training on Legionella risks · Documentation of all monitoring & actions · Report positive cultures to State Health Dept

Common Findings
  • No written WMP; unclear accountability
  • Risk assessment incomplete (missing fountain, humidifier)
  • No sampling schedule or sporadic cultures
  • Positive culture with no corrective action log
🌡️
Hot Water Temperature Monitoring
ASHRAE 188-2018 · Daily storage + Monthly distribution
Temperature Standards

Storage: 140°F (60°C) minimum to kill Legionella · Point of Use: 124°F (51°C) minimum at furthest outlet · Monitoring: daily at tank; monthly at 5+ distribution points

Dead Legs & Flushing

Identify rarely used branches · Flush infrequently used outlets weekly · Install TMVs to prevent scalding (>130°F) · Document flushing dates & temps · Replace stagnant water sections

Common Findings
  • Storage temp <140°F (circulating pump failure)
  • Outlet temps <124°F (long distribution runs)
  • No flushing log for dead legs
  • Monthly outlet temps not recorded
🔗 CDC Legionella Water Management
Water Sample Log
Legionella cultures & biocide testing

Emergency Management
Emergency Codes
IAP Quick Start
ICS-based Incident Action Plan builder — tap to start
HVA Tool
Kaiser Permanente Hazard Vulnerability Analysis — tap to open
Drill Management
Schedule, track, and document emergency drills — tap to log
EOP Reference Hub
Log and track incidents from your Emergency Operations Plan
Emergency Preparedness Program Requirements
📋
Emergency Operations Plan (EOP)
EM.12.01.01 · Annual Review Required
Six Critical Areas

1. Communications — internal notification, staff hotline, media updates
2. Resources & Assets — stockpiles, mutual aid, vendor contacts
3. Safety & Security — evacuation, lockdown, workplace violence response

Continued

4. Staff Roles — IC, section chiefs, department leads, alternates
5. Utilities — emergency power, water, medical gas continuity
6. Patient Care — admission/discharge, medication management during emergency

Key Compliance Points
  • Annual review & revision required (document date)
  • Tested through exercises (2 per year minimum)
  • All key staff trained on their EOP role
  • Mutual aid agreements in writing
🌍
Hazard Vulnerability Analysis (HVA)
EM.12.02.01 · Annual · All-Hazards Approach
Hazard Categories

Natural: earthquakes, floods, hurricanes, tornadoes, winter storms
Technological: power outage, water contamination, hazmat spill, IT failure
Human: mass casualty, pandemic, workplace violence, cyber attack

Scoring

Rate each hazard: Likelihood (1–5) × Severity (1–5) = Risk Score. Higher scores drive resource allocation & EOP priorities. Annual update reflects new hazards & local threat changes.

🎓
Emergency Training & Exercises
EM.12.02.07 · 2 exercises per year minimum
Exercise Requirements

Minimum 2 exercises per calendar year · At least 1 must be community-wide or full-scale (not tabletop-only) · All critical EOP functions tested · Document participation & observer notes

After-Action Process

After-action report (AAR) within 14 days · Identified gaps & improvement opportunities · Corrective action plan with timelines · Track completion · Share findings with entire EM team

🍲
Subsistence Needs — 96-Hour Plan
EM.12.02.04 · Stockpile & Planning
What Must Be Stocked

Food & Water: 3 meals/day + potable water for all staff & patients on-site · Medical Supplies: critical drugs, IV fluids, blood products · Linens & Hygiene: sheets, blankets, toiletries

Continued

Medical Gases: O₂, compressed air, nitrogen backup · EPSS Fuel: 3–7 days minimum · Mutual Aid: agreements for emergency resupply from suppliers & neighboring facilities

🤝
Mutual Aid Agreements
EM.12.02.09 · Formal Partnerships
Partners & Scope

Other hospitals, critical access facilities, outpatient centers, shared services (lab, imaging) · Resources: patient surge capacity, staff, equipment, supplies, utilities (fuel, power)

Agreement Elements

Specific resources offered/requested · Activation triggers & notification process · Cost reimbursement & liability · PHI sharing permissions · Annual review & contact updates · Tested during exercises

🔐
HIPAA Emergency Provisions & §1135 Waivers
EM.13.01.01 · PHI During Emergencies
Waiver Authority

HHS Secretary may issue §1135 waivers during declared disasters, temporarily suspending HIPAA enforcement to allow PHI sharing for emergency response & patient care coordination.

Documentation

Record PHI disclosed under emergency provisions · Note declaration reference · Accreditation may require after-action justification · Return to standard HIPAA controls when emergency ends

Drill & Exercise Log
Emergency Drills & Exercises
Tabletops, full-scale, and community exercises

No drills on record

Joint Commission requires a minimum of 2 emergency exercises per year. At least one must be a community-wide or full-scale exercise. Document each with date, type, participants, objectives, findings, and improvement plan.
Inspections
Central hub for all facility inspection forms.
Use Rounds for department walkthroughs, or launch a targeted inspection form below.
🔥
Fire & Life Safety
Fire doors, exit lights, sprinkler systems, pull stations, extinguishers
⚡
Utilities & EPSS
Generator, ATS, medical gas, electrical panels, HVAC
🏥
Patient Environment
Cleanliness, sharps disposal, infection control, ligature risks
⚗️
Hazardous Materials
Chemical storage, SDS accessibility, waste segregation, spill kits
🔒
Security
Access controls, CCTV, panic buttons, visitor management, key control
🧹
Environmental Services
Terminal cleaning, product usage, isolation precautions compliance
🧺
Annual Linen Vendor Inspection
JC EC.02.02.01 · Annual vendor audit
Quick-Start Checklists
🧯
Monthly Fire Extinguisher Check
NFPA 10 · Every 30 days
  1. Pressure gauge in green zone (visual check)
  2. No visible damage, dents, or corrosion
  3. Accessible & not blocked by equipment or furniture
  4. Pull pin intact and tamper seal unbroken
  5. Inspection tag current (within 12 months for annual, monthly initials)
  6. Hung or mounted securely on bracket/hanger
  7. Correct extinguisher type for area hazard (A/B/C)
  8. No corrosion on discharge nozzle or hose
  9. Handle & trigger mechanism intact
  10. Label legible, operating instructions visible
  11. Location sign visible and unobstructed
  12. Date initialed on inspection tag
⚙️
Weekly Generator Walk-Around
NFPA 110 · Every 7 days
  1. Fuel tank level adequate (≥3/4 tank recommended)
  2. Coolant level visible in sight glass or reservoir
  3. Oil level confirmed via dipstick
  4. Battery voltage adequate (>24V for 24V system)
  5. No visible leaks — oil, fuel, or coolant
  6. Transfer switch confirmed in "AUTO" position
  7. Control panel — no warning lights or active alarms
  8. Alarm beacon & audible alert functional
  9. No debris, rodent activity, or blockage at air intakes
  10. Record: date, fuel level %, inspector name, any anomalies
💡
Monthly Emergency Exit Light Test
NFPA 101 §7.9 & 7.10 · Monthly 30-sec test
  1. All exit signs illuminated (red or green per local code)
  2. No burned-out bulbs or dark panels
  3. Battery backup operational — press test button, confirm both lamps light
  4. Signs visible from 100 feet minimum
  5. No obstructions blocking view of any sign
  6. Fixture secure — not loose, tilted, or damaged
  7. Emergency egress path lighting illuminates adequately
  8. Record date tested, location, pass/fail, any repairs initiated
Annual: Full 90-minute discharge test required. Battery must sustain full illumination throughout.
🚪
Quarterly Fire Door Inspection
NFPA 80 §5.2 · Annual full inspection + quarterly observations
  1. Door self-closes completely without assistance
  2. Door latches fully on close (latch engages strike)
  3. No obstructions in door swing path
  4. Gap at floor <3/4" (or with effective sweep seal)
  5. Gap between stiles ≤1/8" (smoke seals present)
  6. All hinges, latch hardware, and closers intact & functional
  7. No cracks, warping, holes, or damage to door or frame
  8. Fire rating label visible & legible
  9. Magnetic hold-open device (if present) releases on alarm test
  10. Opening force appropriate — not too stiff, not swinging freely
Investigation

Coming Soon

This module is under development.

Risk Assessments
Structured risk assessments required by Joint Commission & CMS.
Document, track, and file completed assessments here. Each becomes part of your compliance record.
Construction & Renovation Risk
🏗️
Pre-Construction Risk Assessment (PCRA)
Required before ANY construction, renovation, or maintenance that may disturb dust or create airborne particles. EC.02.06.05.
🛡️
Infection Control Risk Assessment (ICRA)
Companion to PCRA. Infection prevention assessment of patient population risk during construction. Class I–IV matrix.
Patient & Staff Safety Risk
🧠
Behavioral Health Risk Assessment
Environmental safety for behavioral health areas. Assess ligature risks, sharps, breakables. Required per patient area type and population.
🔒
Security Risk Assessment
Annual review of physical security, access controls, workplace violence prevention per CMS CoP and Joint Commission EC.02.01.01.
Facility Hazard Vulnerability
🌍
Hazard Vulnerability Analysis (HVA)
Annual Kaiser Permanente HVA methodology. Score natural, technological, and human hazards by likelihood × severity. JC EM.12.02.01.
🏥
Environment of Care Risk Assessment
Annual EC risk assessment per EC.01.01.01. Cover safety, security, hazmat, fire, medical equipment, utilities, and emergency management.
Assessment Records
Construction & ICRA Assessments

No assessments on file

BH, Security & HVA Assessments

No assessments on file

Reference
📋
PCRA/ICRA Classification Matrix
EC 02.06.05 · IC 02.02.01
Construction Activity Types

Type A: Inspection/non-invasive work · Type B: Small-scale, short-duration · Type C: Any work that generates moderate-to-high levels of dust · Type D: Major demolition & construction

Patient Risk Groups

Group 1: Low-risk (offices, waiting areas) · Group 2: Medium-risk (med/surg) · Group 3: High-risk (oncology, long-term care) · Group 4: Highest-risk (ICU, OR, bone marrow, NICU)

The Activity Type × Risk Group matrix (4×4=16 combinations) determines Class I–IV infection control measures: HEPA filtration, negative pressure, barriers, staff PPE, and work restrictions.
✅
Joint Commission Assessment Requirements Summary
EC & EM Standards · Annual Cycle
Required Assessments

Safety Risk Assessment (EC.01.01.01) · HVA (EM.12.02.01) · Behavioral Health Environmental Audit · PCRA/ICRA (all construction) · Annual review of findings & action plans

Documentation Required

Date, assessor name, signature · Scope & methodology · Identified risks rated by likelihood × severity · Corrective actions & responsible parties · Follow-up timelines · Evidence of reassessment post-action

Incident Action Plan (IAP)
ICS-Based — Activate and document a response

🔥
Fire & Life Safety Inspection
JC EC.02.03.05 / NFPA 101
Mark each item Pass / Fail / N/A
Fire extinguishers present, tagged, not obstructed
Pull stations unobstructed and accessible
Sprinkler heads clear (18" clearance)
Exit signs illuminated and visible
Exit doors unobstructed and functional
Fire/smoke doors close and latch properly
Corridors ≥8 ft (clinical areas)
No storage in mechanical/electrical rooms
Space heaters absent (unless approved)
Gas cylinders secured and capped
ABHR dispensers properly mounted
Fire alarm panel — normal, no active alerts
⚡
Utilities & EPSS Inspection
JC EC.02.05.07 / NFPA 110
Mark each item Pass / Fail / N/A
Generator fuel level ≥75%
Generator run hours logged
ATS transfer test completed this month
Battery-backed exit lights functional
Medical gas levels in safe range
Medical gas alarms functional
HVAC filter schedule tracked
Electrical panel doors closed and labeled
No exposed wiring in patient areas
No active plumbing leaks
Hot water temp 110–120°F at patient fixtures
Boiler room — access restricted
🏥
Patient Environment Inspection
JC EC.02.06.01 / IC.02.02.01
Mark each item Pass / Fail / N/A
Sharps containers ≤¾ full and mounted
No uncapped sharps visible
Biohazard waste containers labeled, not overfull
Patient rooms clean — no visible soil
High-touch surfaces wiped per protocol
Isolation signs posted correctly
PPE stocked outside isolation rooms
Ligature risks absent
Medications secured, not unattended
IV lines labeled with date
Food items stored and dated
Call light within reach of each patient
⚗️
Hazardous Materials Inspection
JC EC.02.02.01 / OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200
Mark each item Pass / Fail / N/A
SDS available at point of use
All containers labeled (GHS/NFPA)
Incompatible chemicals stored separately
Flammable cabinet in use
Chemo waste labeled and segregated
Pharmaceutical waste in correct containers
Sharps pharmaceutical waste containers present
Spill kits stocked and accessible
Hazmat PPE available
Eyewash flushed within 7 days
No expired chemicals in active use
Hazmat area adequately ventilated
🔒
Security Inspection
JC EC.02.01.01 / CMS CoP §482.13(e)
Mark each item Pass / Fail / N/A
Exterior doors secured / card-access functional
Visitor management active at main entry
All staff wearing visible ID badges
Infant security system active
CCTV cameras operational (spot check)
Panic/duress alarms tested this month
Key/card inventory reconciled
Parking lot well-lit
Security desk staffed or monitored
BH unit — ligature risks documented
Psych seclusion — no contraband
Security incident log current
🧹
Environmental Services Inspection
JC IC.02.02.01 / CDC Environmental Cleaning
Mark each item Pass / Fail / N/A
Terminal cleaning log complete for discharged rooms
EPA-registered disinfectants in use
Disinfectant contact time observed
Mop heads/cloths changed per protocol
Clean and soiled utility rooms separated
Soiled linen in covered hampers
Trash bags not overfull or dragged
No cross-contamination of carts
High-touch surface log complete
OR/procedure rooms — terminal clean documented
C. diff rooms — sporicidal agent used
EVS staff PPE correct for isolation rooms
🏗️

🦠

JC IC.01.03.01

JC EC.02.01.01

JC EM.01.01.01 / CMS CoP

Rate each hazard 0–3: Probability × Impact × Preparedness

HazardProbabilityImpactPreparedness
